nativeRange.habitat: Cultivar of Bucephalandra; the parent genus is endemic to the island of Borneo, where wild populations grow as rheophytes on rocks and submerged wood along fast-flowing rainforest streams and riverbanks.

decorPreferences: lava rock, driftwood or other porous hardscape for attaching the rhizome
gentle to moderate flow across the rhizome, mimicking rheophyte stream conditions
shaded to moderately lit foreground or midground positions
established, stable tank — avoid newly cycled systems

goodWith: Neocaridina and Caridina shrimp that graze biofilm without harming the plant
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Java fern and aquatic mosses
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers (large cichlids) likely to dislodge attached rhizomes
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters

summary: A rare, slow-growing Bucephalandra cultivar from Borneo with elongated, slightly wavy green leaves that develop subtle blue, purple or metallic-silver iridescence under brighter lighting. Treated as a standard epiphyte: attach to rock or wood and never bury the rhizome.
careNotes: Bucephalandra 'Artemis' is a named cultivar of the Borneo-endemic aroid genus Bucephalandra (family Araceae), and its baseline husbandry follows the parent entry (fw-plant-016). Like all Bucephalandra it is a small rheophytic epiphyte whose creeping rhizome is attached to hardscape (lava rock, driftwood, slate) with cyanoacrylate glue gel, thread or fishing line and left fully exposed to the water column; burying the rhizome is generally reported to cause it to rot. Cultivar-specific traits documented by trade sources: leaves are small, elongated and slightly wavy, with a characteristic 'palm tree'-like arrangement along the rhizome that distinguishes Artemis from broader-leaved cultivars such as 'Brownie' or 'Kedagang'; coloration is predominantly vibrant to dark green with subtle hints of blue, purple or a metallic silver sheen that comes out most strongly under moderate to higher lighting (a structural-color effect typical of the genus). Reported water parameters across the two trade sources cluster at roughly 21-28 °C and pH ~6.0-8.0; the conservative pH window 6.0-7.5 used here matches the genus-level consensus. Lighting is flexible: low to medium light is sufficient for healthy slow growth, and brighter light enhances coloration but tends to invite algae on the slow-growing leaves if not paired with CO2. CO2 supplementation is optional but visibly improves growth rate and color. Growth is very slow, and Artemis is reported to be especially slow even by Bucephalandra standards (which is why it is typically sold in small 1-2 inch portions). Nutrients are taken up from the water column, so substrate is irrelevant and light liquid fertilization is sufficient. Like other Bucephalandra, Artemis can show 'buce melt' when first transitioned from emersed to submersed growth or after sharp changes in temperature, pH or TDS; as long as the rhizome itself stays firm, new submersed-form leaves typically regrow from the rhizome over the following weeks. Best introduced into established, stable tanks.
breedingNotes: Propagated in the hobby by rhizome division: once Artemis is established and putting out new leaves and roots, cut the rhizome at a natural bend with clean sharp scissors so each division carries several leaves and some roots, then reattach the new piece to its own piece of hardscape. Side shoots produced by the rhizome over time provide convenient natural division points. Sexual reproduction via the genus' spathe/spadix inflorescence is occasionally observed on emersed plants but is not a practical hobby method.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Primary source. Documents Borneo riverbank origin, low-to-moderate lighting (brighter enhances color), optional CO2, temperature 70-82°F (21-28°C), slow epiphyte growth, sold in 1-2 inch portions, foreground-to-midground placement, propagation by rhizome division with side shoots, easy care, rhizome must never be buried, vibrant green slightly wavy leaves with subtle blue/purple hints under good lighting.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked Borneo endemism, low-to-medium lighting (metallic silver sheen under brighter light), CO2 optional but recommended for faster growth, pH 6.0-8.0, slow growth, small elongated wavy leaves in a palm tree-like formation, dark green with iridescent highlights, foreground-to-midground placement, propagation by rhizome division with sharp scissors, easy care, epiphyte (attach to rock/wood, remove rockwool, never bury rhizome), tolerates submersed or emersed growth, sensitive to drastic parameter changes.

additional.name: Bucephalandra genus entry (AquaMate fw-plant-016)
additional.notes: Used for shared genus-level husbandry baselines (water-parameter consensus, structural-color iridescence mechanism, buce-melt behavior, propagation conventions). Sourced from Tropica, Aquarium Co-Op, 2HR Aquarist, Aquariadise and AquariumBreeder per that entry's cited sources.
